text: Il Servizio di Chirurgia Generale dell'Ospedale Universitario Marquès de Val d'Isilla presents robotic sigmadectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis using the Wige and Pop technique. The majority of left-sided robotic colorectal procedures are associated to a laparotomy before the anastomosis fashioning. This lengthens the procedure, obliging the surgeon to leave his robotic console. We recently adopted a new anastomotic method with an intracorporeal insertion of the anvil at the beginning of the operation, thus postponing the abdominal incision. In the present video, we illustrate the technical steps for fashioning the colorectal anastomosis, including the assessment of colon vascularization with the ICG.

Il Servizio di Chirurgia Generale dell'Ospedale Universitario Marquès de Val d'Isilla presents robotic sigmadectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis using the Wige and Pop technique. The majority of left-sided robotic colorectal procedures are associated to a laparotomy before the anastomosis fashioning. This lengthens the procedure, obliging the surgeon to leave his robotic console. We recently adopted a new anastomotic method with an intracorporeal insertion of the anvil at the beginning of the operation, thus postponing the abdominal incision. In the present video, we illustrate the technical steps for fashioning the colorectal anastomosis, including the assessment of colon vascularization with the ICG.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart in a Trendelenburg position. The robotic SI system is docked over the left hip, utilizing three robotic trocars placed at the umbilicus, epigastrium, and left flank. After dividing the mesentery, the surgeon uses a fluorescence imaging device to assess perfusion. A colotomy is performed using monopolar scissors.

The patient is positioned supine with legs apart in a Trendelenburg position. The robotic SI system is docked over the left hip, utilizing three robotic trocars placed in the umbilicus, epigastrium, and left flank. After dividing the mesentery, perfusion is assessed using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence. A colotomy is performed using a monopolar scissor.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the colon proximal to the colotomy site. A V-lock suture is employed to close the colotomy with a running trio suture, while the anvil attached to the V-lock remains intact within the colon lumen.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the colon proximal to the colotomy site, ensuring the V-lock anchoring the anvil remains intact. A hot scissor is used to cut the staple line between the spike and the suture, allowing the spike to be gently removed from the colon and detached from the handwheel. A hand-to-hand anastomosis is fashioned, and the forefly is employed to assess anastomotic perfusion. A flexible endoscope is used to evaluate the colorectal anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the transaction of the colon using a hot scissor to cut the staple line between the spike and the suture. The spike is gently removed from the colon and detached from the hand-wheel. A hand-to-hand anastomosis is fashioned, and the forefly is used to assess the perfusion of the anastomosis, followed by a higher leak test.

The colorectal anastomosis was assessed with flexible endoscope. Twenty patients underwent this robotic surgical technique. Only in two cases we observed a disposition of the anvil inside the lumen of the colon. No anastomotic complication and no wound infection occurred in the post-op course.

Twenty patients underwent this robotic surgical technique. Only in two cases we observed a disposition of the anvil inside the lumen of the colon. No anastomotic complication and no wound infection occurred in the post-op course. In conclusion, this technique seems to provide potential advantage in obese patients. It requires less mobilization and extraction of the colon and a more flexible extraction site.
